whatsNew.title: Royal Ascot
whatsNew.summary: Dress notes have been refined for the 2026 season.

whatsNew.title: Henley Royal Regatta
whatsNew.summary: Access guidance has been tightened for house parties, enclosures, and timing.

whatsNew.title: Paris Haute Couture Week
whatsNew.summary: The briefing is now live for the upcoming season.

whatsNew.title: Season Notes
whatsNew.summary: Several spring briefings have been tightened for clarity and quicker reading.

events.title: White Tie
events.summary: The highest standard of Western formal dress, reserved for state dinners, royal galas, and ceremonial evenings of the first order. It rewards absolute correctness and admits no approximation.

metadata.typicalGuests: Heads of state, royalty, senior diplomats

atAGlance.men: Black tailcoat, white waistcoat, white self-tied bow tie, patent shoes
atAGlance.women: Full-length gown, formal shoes, considered jewellery
atAGlance.avoid: Dinner jackets, black bow ties, wristwatches, early departure

men: Black tailcoat with silk-faced peak lapels
Patent pumps or highly polished patent oxfords
women: Tiara only where entitled or specifically invited

protocol.behavior: Stand until the host or senior dignitary is seated. Manner should be composed and conversation measured throughout.
protocol.dining: Port is passed to the left without exception. Do not drink before the principal toast where a formal sequence applies.
protocol.digital: Phones remain out of sight entirely. Photography and social posting are not appropriate.
protocol.forms_of_address: The Sovereign: 'Your Majesty', then 'Sir' or 'Ma'am'. A Duke or Duchess: 'Your Grace'.

commonMistakes: Wearing a wristwatch
Using any black tie component
Showing too much waistcoat above the tailcoat
Leaving before the host
Producing a phone in view
checklist: Phone is silent and out of sight

events.title: Black Tie
events.summary: The standard for formal dinners, galas, and evening receptions. Black tie creates a disciplined visual backdrop; the occasion takes precedence, and the individual dresses accordingly.

metadata.venue: Hotels, private dining rooms, formal venues
metadata.typicalGuests: Corporate leaders, social figures, public personalities

atAGlance.men: Dinner jacket, white evening shirt, black self-tied bow tie, polished black shoes
atAGlance.women: Evening gown or a well-judged cocktail dress with refined accessories
atAGlance.avoid: Pre-tied bow ties, brown shoes, casual fabrics, late arrival without notice

men: Black or midnight blue dinner jacket
Black cummerbund or low evening waistcoat
women: Elegant cocktail dress where the room permits

protocol.behavior: Arrive shortly after the stated time. Greetings should be easy, composed, and host-led.
protocol.dining: Cutlery from the outside in. Bread broken by hand into small pieces.
protocol.digital: Phones stay silent and off the table. They are not consulted during a course.
protocol.forms_of_address: Knights are addressed as 'Sir' with the first name. Peers below duke are 'Lord' or 'Lady'.

commonMistakes: Wearing a pre-tied bow tie
Substituting a business suit
Eating before the host
Consulting a phone at table
Arriving notably late without notice
checklist: Jacket is pressed and well fitted

events.title: Royal Garden Party
events.summary: A formal royal occasion held to recognise public service and distinguished contribution. It combines elegance with practicality and asks guests to observe royal etiquette throughout.

metadata.typicalGuests: Members of the Royal Family, honourees, invited guests

atAGlance.men: Morning dress, tailored lounge suit, uniform, or formal national dress
atAGlance.women: Formal day dress or trouser suit with hat or fascinator
atAGlance.avoid: Stilettos on grass, casual wear, uninvited address, selfies

men: Morning dress or well-tailored lounge suit
Top hat optional with morning dress
women: Formal day dress at or below the knee
Low block heels or flats suited to the lawn

protocol.behavior: Wait to be addressed before speaking to a member of the Royal Family. Keep any exchange brief, respectful, and composed.
protocol.dining: Manage tea, plate, and conversation without crowding service points.
protocol.digital: Photography in the gardens may be permitted. Selfies with members of the Royal Family are not appropriate.
protocol.forms_of_address: The Sovereign: 'Your Majesty' first, then 'Sir' or 'Ma'am'. Other senior royals: 'Your Royal Highness' first.

commonMistakes: Wearing stilettos on the lawn
Approaching a royal without being addressed first
Treating the occasion as an informal garden event
Requesting a photograph
Disregarding the outdoor setting in dress choices
checklist: Shoes are suited to the lawn
Correct forms of address are known

events.title: Business Formal Dinner
events.summary: A professional dining occasion where self-command, table conduct, and conversational judgement are visible at once. It asks for polish without performance and ease without informality.

metadata.venue: Private dining rooms, high-end restaurants, board settings

atAGlance.men: Dark suit, crisp shirt, considered tie, polished shoes
atAGlance.women: Business suit, conservative dress, or refined trouser suit
atAGlance.avoid: Over-ordering, phone use at table, unsolicited business proposals, asking for the remainder to take away

women: A polished appearance free of glamour

protocol.behavior: Rise for introductions. Follow the host's lead on seating, conversation pace, and the close of the evening.
protocol.dining: Bread left, meal centre, water right. Avoid dishes that require effort or create mess.
protocol.digital: Phones remain off the table and on silent. Full attention belongs to those present.
protocol.forms_of_address: Courtesy titles and surnames unless a more familiar address is invited. Senior officials may require office-based forms.

commonMistakes: Ordering the most expensive item on the menu
Consulting a phone during the meal
Beginning before the host
Using the dinner as an occasion for a hard pitch
Asking to take the remainder away
checklist: Attire is a step above office standard
Phone is put away on arrival
Food choice is neat and considered

events.title: Private Members Club
events.summary: A private members club is a discreet refuge from public life. Its rules exist to protect privacy, preserve atmosphere, and return the advantage to those who understand the room.

metadata.typicalGuests: Members, patrons, cultural and professional figures

atAGlance.men: Tailored jacket, collared shirt, polished shoes; tie often required
atAGlance.women: Elegant smart attire such as a dress or tailored trouser suit
atAGlance.avoid: Denim, trainers, raised voices, photography, spreading business papers

men: No denim, trainers, shorts, or sportswear
women: Elegant dress or tailored trouser suit
No denim, shorts, or casual footwear

protocol.behavior: Review house rules before visiting. Move quietly. If attending as a guest, your conduct reflects on the member who invited you.
protocol.discretion: Do not approach public figures for conversation unless introduced. Business is not conducted in social rooms.
protocol.digital: Devices remain silent. Calls are confined to designated areas. Photography is commonly prohibited.
protocol.forms_of_address: Courtesy titles remain appropriate unless a more familiar relationship is clearly established.

commonMistakes: Disregarding house dress rules
Spreading business papers in social rooms
Taking photographs
Speaking at a volume that carries
Treating the club as a hotel lobby

events.title: Diplomatic Reception
events.summary: A diplomatic reception is governed by hierarchy, tact, and international courtesy. Correct forms of address and careful observation are not optional refinements; they are the minimum expected.

metadata.typicalGuests: Ambassadors, high commissioners, envoys, senior officials

atAGlance.men: Dark suit unless the invitation specifies otherwise
atAGlance.women: Formal or semi-formal dress suited to the invitation
atAGlance.avoid: Guessing at titles, commenting on the host country's affairs, treating it as a social cocktail party

men: Dark formal suit unless the invitation specifies uniform, black tie, or national dress
women: Formal or semi-formal dress for evening functions
Refined day dress for daytime occasions
Attire scaled to the custom of the host country

protocol.behavior: Move through receiving lines without delay. Precedence governs introductions and seating.
protocol.dining: At standing receptions, take small portions and move away from the service points promptly.
protocol.digital: Phones remain discreet, silent, and subordinate to the occasion.
protocol.forms_of_address: Ambassadors and high commissioners: 'Your Excellency' in formal address, or 'Mr Ambassador' and 'Madam Ambassador' in direct conversation.

commonMistakes: Disregarding the dress code on the invitation
Applying titles carelessly or incorrectly
Commenting on the host country's internal affairs
Arriving late to a seated function
Using familiar or casual language in formal address
checklist: Dress code confirmed from the invitation

occasions.name: Milan Fashion Week Men's
occasions.access_type: Industry invitations, showroom appointments, selected public events
occasions.dress_code: Sharp day tailoring; restraint reads better than trend-chasing
occasions.guest_mix: Buyers, editors, designers, luxury clients, brand partners
occasions.summary: The January menswear shows open the European fashion calendar with a rhythm built around appointments, presentations, and a great deal of silent judgment in hotel lobbies.
occasions.why_it_matters: Milan rewards those who understand that being fashionably dressed and being correctly dressed for the room are not the same achievement.
occasions.the_room: Editors, buyers, VIC clients, show producers, PR teams, and brand hosts moving between catwalks, salons, and hotel-lobby triage.
dress_notes: Treat daytime as business dressing sharpened by fashion literacy, not costume.
Outerwear, bag, and shoes are read before anyone checks the invitation list.
Choose pieces that can move from a show bench to a private fitting without looking overworked.
what_to_get_right: Know whether you are seated, standing, or arriving only for a showroom appointment.
Take your assigned place quickly; hesitation at the bench is instantly visible.
Post-show congratulations should be brief unless a house team clearly keeps you back.
before_you_go: Group venues by district; traffic between shows is part of the day, not an exception.
Keep QR codes, guest names, and showroom confirmations in one place.
Book lunches and dinners early; the strongest rooms are effectively full before the week starts.
occasions.preview_summary: The January menswear shows open the European fashion calendar with a rhythm built around appointments, presentations, and a great deal of silent judgment in hotel lobbies.
occasions.premium_briefing: The shows matter, but the real currency sits in showroom appointments, private client lunches, and whether a house bothered to place you on a list rather than in a holding line.
seen_online: Most posts are about street style outside the venue. The more useful clue is whether the brand posts you from inside the show or showroom.
A pavement photo means you made it to Milan. A seat card means Milan noticed.
If the outfit is shouting louder than the invitation, the ranking may be lower than the caption suggests.

occasions.name: World Economic Forum Annual Meeting
occasions.venue: Congress Centre and private venues across town
occasions.access_type: Accredited delegates, invited partners, private house guests
occasions.dress_code: Quiet winter tailoring with alpine practicality
occasions.guest_mix: Heads of state, CEOs, policy advisers, financiers, editors
occasions.summary: Davos is less a conference than a temporary court of power, where scheduling discipline, introductions, and discretion matter as much as any panel title.
occasions.why_it_matters: The etiquette of Davos is about time, hierarchy, and confidentiality. Those who overplay access quickly reveal that they do not have it.
occasions.the_room: Heads of state, CEOs, ministers, policy staff, foundation leaders, editors, and a shadow calendar of chalet breakfasts and closed-door dinners.
dress_notes: Dress for snow, security queues, and walking between venues rather than a chauffeured fantasy.
Technical outerwear is acceptable if it stays dark, clean, and disciplined.
Wear badges exactly where required, then remove them once you are off duty and off site.
what_to_get_right: Treat security windows and session starts as hard deadlines, not advisory ones.
Private houses are not networking free-for-alls; enter on a host name, not optimism.
Nothing from an off-record conversation becomes a dinner anecdote later.
before_you_go: Build each day around geography as well as calendar time; Davos punishes backtracking.
Confirm security requirements and credential rules for every venue on your schedule.
Carry boots, power, and business cards, but use all three without display.
occasions.preview_summary: Davos is less a conference than a temporary court of power, where scheduling discipline, introductions, and discretion matter as much as any panel title.
occasions.premium_briefing: Badges decide more than charm. The decisive move is reaching the right security zone on time, then understanding which conversation is public, on background, or simply not yours to mention later.
seen_online: Online Davos is full of snow, badges, and stage photos. The stronger signal is breakfast in a chalet or a quiet dinner away from the main halls.
A lanyard proves entry. A small off-camera group proves relevance.
The polished Davos move is posting less and implying more. The room is already keeping score.

occasions.name: Snow Polo World Cup St. Moritz
occasions.access_type: Ticketed grandstand, hospitality, invited guests
occasions.host_type: Snow Polo World Cup St. Moritz
occasions.dress_code: Luxe alpine daywear with weather discipline
occasions.guest_mix: St. Moritz regulars, sponsors, private clients, international guests
occasions.summary: Played on the frozen lake, Snow Polo is one of the clearest examples of Alpine society at full gloss: sport in the foreground, hospitality and observation just behind it.
occasions.why_it_matters: It is a cold-weather occasion that still rewards polish. Looking prepared rather than overdressed is the mark to hit.
occasions.the_room: St. Moritz regulars, sponsor hosts, private-bank clients, polo patrons, and weekend guests moving between lake edge, tented hospitality, and late lunch tables.
dress_notes: Choose luxe alpine layers that still look composed once you step into a heated tent.
Footwear needs grip on ice and enough polish for sponsor hospitality.
Sun glare on the lake is real; sunglasses should feel refined rather than sporty.
what_to_get_right: Arrive before the first chukka or you spend the best social window catching up to the room.
Respect seated sightlines in hospitality; standing at the rail for too long is noticed.
Hosts and sponsors matter here; thank the table before drifting.
before_you_go: Confirm whether you have hospitality, grandstand, or sponsor access; they shape the day differently.
Reserve lunch and dinner early; the town books around polo weekend.
Check weather and lake conditions the night before, then dress one degree warmer than seems stylish.
occasions.preview_summary: Played on the frozen lake, Snow Polo is one of the clearest examples of Alpine society at full gloss: sport in the foreground, hospitality and observation just behind it.
occasions.premium_briefing: The strongest positions are not necessarily at the rail but at the right lunch table. Snow Polo rewards guests who can move from ice to hospitality without looking either underprepared or theatrically alpine.
seen_online: The internet loves glossy lake-side glamour. The better clue is who hosts your table once everyone goes inside to thaw.
A frozen-lake portrait says you attended. A sponsor lunch says you were expected.
If someone looks equally comfortable on the ice and in the tent, they probably know the weekend rather well.

occasions.name: Paris Haute Couture Week
occasions.access_type: Maison invitation, press accreditation, client appointments
occasions.host_type: Federation de la Haute Couture et de la Mode
occasions.dress_code: Refined day dressing with couture-level finish
occasions.guest_mix: Maison clients, editors, stylists, collectors, luxury executives
occasions.summary: Haute Couture Week is Paris at its most ceremonial in fashion terms: fewer rooms, more weight, and a higher premium on ease, judgment, and access.
occasions.why_it_matters: Couture rooms are sensitive to proportion. Loud dressing, loud conversation, and loud ambition all read badly.
occasions.the_room: Maison clients, couture salon teams, editors, stylists, celebrity dressers, and guests who know exactly which door is for show seating and which is for fittings.
dress_notes: Clothes should look resolved rather than merely expensive.
Jewellery and accessories should never compete with the collection or the salon.
Hair, shoes, and coat are part of the invitation test long before a greeting.
what_to_get_right: Follow the seating card exactly; couture rooms remember uncertainty.
Keep phones down once a fitting or show begins; houses notice divided attention.
Backstage access, atelier visits, and second looks are granted, not improvised.
before_you_go: Separate show invitations from salon appointments; they are not interchangeable access.
Leave margin for Paris traffic and security even on short cross-town hops.
Confirm fittings, cars, and any post-show return window in writing.
occasions.preview_summary: Haute Couture Week is Paris at its most ceremonial in fashion terms: fewer rooms, more weight, and a higher premium on ease, judgment, and access.
occasions.premium_briefing: Couture is not about being seen at the door; it is about being expected in the salon. Houses remember who protects intimacy, respects house PR, and understands that the real access point is the private appointment.
seen_online: Most posts focus on front rows and dramatic exits. The stronger signal is being shown in a salon fitting or private house interior.
A doorway photo says fashion week. A maison appointment says access.
The quiet rule of couture online: the less someone waves the invitation around, the more likely it was real.

occasions.name: The I.C.E. St. Moritz
occasions.access_type: Ticketed access, partner hospitality, invited collectors
occasions.dress_code: Elegant winter dressing with practical footwear
occasions.guest_mix: Collectors, automotive houses, luxury partners, St. Moritz regulars
occasions.summary: The I.C.E. brings exceptional classic cars onto the frozen lake and turns an automotive concours into one of the season's most photogenic social gatherings.
occasions.why_it_matters: It is a collector event disguised as spectacle. Knowing when to admire and when to step back is part of getting it right.
occasions.the_room: Collectors, owners, marque representatives, concours judges, partner hosts, and St. Moritz regulars who know which conversations are about the cars and which are about each other.
dress_notes: Think elegant winter daywear with enough structure for photographs and enough warmth for the lake.
Gloves, eyewear, and outerwear should feel considered rather than borrowed for effect.
Footwear needs grip; slipping on ice is less charming than people pretend.
what_to_get_right: Do not crowd owners or cars for a closer photograph; patience reads better than enthusiasm.
Respect marshals and barriers immediately; concours etiquette is part safety, part class marker.
Ask before touching anything, even when the owner seems relaxed.
before_you_go: Check whether your day includes preview, concours judging, or the public programme.
Build lunch plans around the schedule rather than assuming you can drift.
Allow extra time for weather, shuttle routing, and lake access changes.
occasions.preview_summary: The I.C.E. brings exceptional classic cars onto the frozen lake and turns an automotive concours into one of the season's most photogenic social gatherings.
occasions.premium_briefing: The spectacle is public; the collector conversation is not. The I.C.E. rewards guests who understand how to admire rare machinery without behaving as if rarity entitles them to proximity.
seen_online: The feed is full of beautiful cars on a beautiful lake. The better clue is whether someone is photographed with owners, judges, or hosts instead of just beside the bonnet.
Anyone can love the scenery. Not everyone gets close enough to stop pretending they are a photographer.
If the post feels calm rather than breathless, that is usually a good sign.

occasions.name: Piarist Debutante Ball
occasions.access_type: Ticketed gala and invited tables
occasions.dress_code: White tie atmosphere with formal evening dress expected
occasions.guest_mix: Debutante families, patrons, committee members, New York society guests
occasions.summary: A formal New York winter ball with charitable purpose and old-world formality, the Piarist Ball still values manners, introductions, and the choreography of the room.
occasions.why_it_matters: Debutante evenings operate on visible courtesy. Family standing, patronage, and generosity all sit close to the surface.
occasions.the_room: Committee families, patrons, clergy-linked supporters, table hosts, and young women whose introductions are being watched as closely as their gowns.
dress_notes: Treat the evening as formal ceremony first and party second.
Jewellery should read finished and family-aware rather than nightclub bright.
Men should clarify tails versus dark formal evening dress with their specific host.
what_to_get_right: Stand promptly for entrances, prayers, toasts, and any formal acknowledgments.
Meet your table host and neighbouring families before ranging further across the room.
Remember the charitable purpose; performative indifference to the cause reads badly.
before_you_go: Confirm seating, arrival window, and whether your host expects a receiving-line greeting.
Review the programme so you do not miss the opening sequence by lingering at cocktails.
Keep cards discreet; this is still a family and philanthropic room before it is a networking room.
occasions.preview_summary: A formal New York winter ball with charitable purpose and old-world formality, the Piarist Ball still values manners, introductions, and the choreography of the room.
occasions.premium_briefing: Debutante balls reward visible courtesy. The useful guests are the ones who can honour family hierarchy, philanthropic purpose, and ceremonial pacing without looking as though they have come to transact.
seen_online: Online coverage leans toward gowns, staircases, and formal lineups. The stronger clue is who is tied to the host tables, families, and charitable side of the evening.
A ballroom photo says glamour. A family or committee mention says standing.
If the caption remembers the cause as well as the dress, someone at the table probably had manners.

occasions.name: White Turf St. Moritz
occasions.access_type: Public racing grounds, hospitality, partner invitations
occasions.dress_code: Winter race-day dressing with real weather tolerance
occasions.guest_mix: Racing crowd, St. Moritz residents, sponsors, weekend guests
occasions.summary: White Turf combines racing, hospitality, and St. Moritz spectacle across three Sundays, with the frozen lake acting as both sporting venue and social stage.
occasions.why_it_matters: The occasion rewards those who understand that proper race-day polish still has to survive cold air, snow, and long hours outdoors.
occasions.the_room: Owners, punters, sponsors, local regulars, and Sunday guests moving between outdoor rail positions, marquees, and long lunches.
dress_notes: Dress for the lake first and the photograph second.
Tailored winter layers, gloves, and proper eyewear outperform anything fragile or fussy.
A serious coat is part of the look; carrying discomfort for style only reads inexperienced.
what_to_get_right: Know which Sunday matters for your hosts; each weekend draws a slightly different crowd.
Keep pathways clear around stands, betting points, and hospitality entries.
Betting talk lands best when it is light and informed, not overperformed.
before_you_go: Confirm whether you have grounds access, hospitality, or a sponsor table.
Plan transport early if you are staying outside central St. Moritz.
Expect glare, cold, and long periods outdoors even if lunch is indoors.
occasions.preview_summary: White Turf combines racing, hospitality, and St. Moritz spectacle across three Sundays, with the frozen lake acting as both sporting venue and social stage.
occasions.premium_briefing: White Turf is a sporting day that quietly sorts the practiced from the theatrical. The guests who get it right are warm enough, punctual enough, and socially light enough to stay elegant through three hours on a frozen lake.
seen_online: Most posts show racing drama, winter coats, and champagne in the cold. The better clue is whether the same hosts keep appearing around the same person each Sunday.
One stylish race-day post means attendance. Repeated hospitality context means the weekend may actually know your name.
The feed loves spectacle. The room still prefers people who can find lunch without making it a saga.

occasions.name: Vienna Opera Ball
occasions.access_type: Ticketed entry; boxes and loges by prior arrangement
occasions.visibility: Public ticketed event with tiered access
occasions.dress_code: White tie without exception
occasions.guest_mix: Austrian society, European aristocracy, diplomats, arts patrons
occasions.focus: White tie and Viennese ball season
occasions.summary: The Opera Ball remains one of the rare evenings in Europe where white tie is not nostalgic styling but live protocol, observed closely and enforced without apology.
occasions.why_it_matters: It is a room in which correctness still outranks flair. Guests who understand the sequence of the evening move differently from those who merely bought a ticket.
occasions.the_room: Austrian society, diplomats, industrial families, international guests, and staff who enforce protocol with more precision than glamour photography suggests.
dress_notes: Men need proper white tie, not a dinner-jacket approximation.
Women should think floor-length elegance, gloves, and composure rather than fashion theatre.
Cloakroom logistics matter, so choose formalwear that still travels well through checkpoints and staircases.
what_to_get_right: Be in place before the opening ceremony; late movement is conspicuous.
Keep movement minimal during formal sequences and respect box or loge boundaries.
Treat photography and phone use as peripheral privileges, not part of the evening.
before_you_go: Confirm ticket type, entrance, cloakroom, and any box arrangements well in advance.
Arrange late transport before the evening begins; departures are slow and formal.
Check every white-tie detail before leaving the hotel, not in the opera stairwell.
occasions.preview_summary: The Opera Ball remains one of the rare evenings in Europe where white tie is not nostalgic styling but live protocol, observed closely and enforced without apology.
occasions.premium_briefing: The visible glamour hides a highly regulated evening: dress is inspected, movement during the opening is conspicuous, and the guests who look most at ease are usually the ones who prepared for the protocol rather than the photographs.
seen_online: Online Vienna is all staircase shots and white tie theatre. The subtler clue is being seen correctly placed inside a box, loge, or formal receiving line.
A grand entrance is nice. Looking as though you have done this before is better.
If no one seems to be adjusting gloves, searching for seats, or negotiating their bow tie, that is usually the higher tier.

occasions.name: Cannes Film Festival
occasions.venue: Palais des Festivals and Riviera venues
occasions.access_type: Accreditation, invitation, ticketed peripheral events, hospitality
occasions.dress_code: Black tie at night; polished Riviera day dressing by day
occasions.guest_mix: Film talent, executives, publicists, patrons, luxury partners
occasions.summary: Cannes is a festival, a market, and a social test conducted under Mediterranean light. Proper conduct is mostly about timing, dress discipline, and reading which invitation actually matters.
occasions.why_it_matters: The red carpet is the visible part. The real calendar is built from screenings, lunches, yacht appointments, and dinners that run on hierarchy rather than noise.
occasions.the_room: Talent, studio executives, publicists, patrons, sales agents, and luxury-brand guests split between the Palais, the market, beach clubs, yachts, and late dinners.
dress_notes: Evening dress should clear premiere formality without tipping into costume.
Daytime Riviera dressing works best when clean, heat-aware, and car-to-meeting ready.
Choose shoes for stairs, carpets, and Croisette walking; Cannes punishes ornamental discomfort.
what_to_get_right: Know the difference between a festival badge, a screening invitation, a market meeting, and a party list.
Do not clog arrival lines, staircases, or yacht gangways for photographs.
Publicists and assistants run the real traffic; treating them well is practical, not merely polite.
before_you_go: Map the Croisette, the Palais, and your tender or driver timings before day one.
Sort credentials and invitations by morning, afternoon, and evening; the city runs on overlapping calendars.
Book dinners early and leave margin between events; Cannes traffic and security swallow optimism.
occasions.preview_summary: Cannes is a festival, a market, and a social test conducted under Mediterranean light. Proper conduct is mostly about timing, dress discipline, and reading which invitation actually matters.
occasions.premium_briefing: Cannes rewards guests who separate access from atmosphere. A red carpet appearance may be the least strategic part of the day compared with a breakfast meeting, market screening, or correctly placed dinner.
seen_online: The red carpet dominates the internet. The better signal is a screening badge, market meeting, yacht lunch, or dinner that sits on the real calendar behind the carpet.
A Croisette pose says presence. A stacked day of invitations says access.
If someone posts less about reaching the carpet, they may already be late for the more important lunch.

occasions.name: Monaco Grand Prix
occasions.venue: Circuit de Monaco and harbour hospitality
occasions.access_type: Grandstand tickets, terraces, yacht hospitality, private hosting
occasions.dress_code: Smart Riviera dressing by day; polished eveningwear after dark
occasions.guest_mix: Racing principals, yacht hosts, luxury clients, celebrities, long-time Monaco regulars
occasions.summary: Monaco Grand Prix weekend is as much about terraces, tenders, and invitations as it is about racing. The calendar rewards precision more than exuberance.
occasions.why_it_matters: Monaco compresses hierarchy into a small geography. Which balcony, yacht, or dinner you are on says more than how loudly you arrive.
occasions.the_room: Team principals, yacht hosts, brokers, sponsor guests, luxury clients, and Monaco regulars circulating between terraces, boats, pits, and dinners.
dress_notes: Daywear should stay sharp in heat while surviving docks, tenders, and steps.
Evening looks need polish without overt theatre; Monaco sees too much of that already.
Footwear must work aboard and ashore.
what_to_get_right: Treat yacht invitations as exact, personal, and non-transferable.
Arrive when your host asks; tender slots and security timings are tighter than they look.
Thank crew and household staff as naturally as you greet the owner or principal.
before_you_go: Confirm each venue with a host name, berth or terrace details, and the right arrival route.
Plan around tender timings, road closures, and tunnel traffic rather than against them.
Book tables, cars, and salon appointments well ahead; the principality runs out of ease that week.
occasions.preview_summary: Monaco Grand Prix weekend is as much about terraces, tenders, and invitations as it is about racing. The calendar rewards precision more than exuberance.
occasions.premium_briefing: Monaco compresses hierarchy into a few streets and a harbour. The useful question is rarely whether you are in Monaco, but whose deck, whose terrace, and whose lunch you are actually on.
seen_online: Online Monaco is yachts, pit-lane selfies, and harbour sparkle. The better clue is exactly whose deck, terrace, or table appears in the post.
A nice backdrop can be rented by geography. A named host is harder to fake.
If the caption includes tender timing, someone probably had a real schedule and not just a very good angle.

occasions.name: Royal Ascot
occasions.access_type: Public enclosures; Royal Enclosure by sponsored membership application
occasions.dress_code: Morning dress in Royal Enclosure; rules are actively enforced
occasions.guest_mix: English society, racing professionals, international guests
occasions.summary: Five days of racing under royal patronage, Royal Ascot remains one of the few public occasions in Britain where dress discipline still has real consequence.
occasions.why_it_matters: The distinction between being admitted and appearing correct is especially sharp at Ascot. Enclosure etiquette still functions as a social language.
occasions.the_room: Enclosure regulars, sponsors, racing professionals, hospitality hosts, and international guests navigating one of the clearest dress and access hierarchies in Britain.
dress_notes: Read your enclosure rules rather than trusting hearsay or last year's photographs.
A hat is not optional where the code says hat, and morning dress should be correct rather than playful.
Choose footwear that can last a long day on racecourse ground without looking pragmatic in the wrong way.
what_to_get_right: Be in place before the Royal Procession; social forgiveness narrows after it begins.
Respect enclosure boundaries and the tone set by your host table.
Treat champagne and betting as part of the day, not its entire personality.
before_you_go: Confirm membership, guest badges, and dining plans early.
Build transport around both arrival and departure; Ascot tests patience on both ends.
Check weather, then adapt within the code rather than trying to negotiate around it.
occasions.preview_summary: Five days of racing under royal patronage, Royal Ascot remains one of the few public occasions in Britain where dress discipline still has real consequence.
occasions.premium_briefing: Ascot is less forgiving than it looks. A hemline, hat, or lapel that slips outside enclosure rules, or a guest who misses the Procession because lunch overran, marks itself immediately.
seen_online: Most posts focus on hats, carriages, and race-day dressing. The stronger clue is calm Royal Enclosure context rather than excitement outside it.
A good outfit says effort. The right enclosure says admission.
If a guest looks pleased but not surprised to be there, that is usually the better sign.

occasions.name: Munich Opera Festival
occasions.access_type: Public ticketing, patron access, invited receptions
occasions.dress_code: Elegant evening dress; black tie only where specified
occasions.guest_mix: Opera patrons, visiting cultural guests, Munich society, international audiences
occasions.summary: Munich's opera festival offers weeks of serious cultural programming with a social calendar built around performances, receptions, and long-established patron habits.
occasions.why_it_matters: Unlike a single gala, the festival rewards consistency. One can appear correctly over a season or one can look as though one dressed only for opening night.
occasions.the_room: Opera patrons, Munich society, visiting cultural travellers, donor circles, and guests moving between performances, receptions, and summer terraces.
dress_notes: Evening elegance should feel considered but not stiff.
Save black tie for invitations that explicitly call for it; most festival evenings reward polish over ceremony.
A coat or wrap still matters for late departures and post-performance receptions.
what_to_get_right: Understand whether you are attending an opening, a regular festival performance, or a donor evening; the room shifts with the date.
Use intervals for conversation, not noisy circulation or last-minute seat trading.
If hosted, follow your host's lead on where to go after curtain call.
before_you_go: Check the exact venue and running time; the festival uses more than one stage and some long evenings.
Book supper and transport around curtain times rather than hoping for flexibility.
Keep tickets and any reception details together; the festival calendar overlaps.
occasions.preview_summary: Munich's opera festival offers weeks of serious cultural programming with a social calendar built around performances, receptions, and long-established patron habits.
occasions.premium_briefing: Munich rewards season-long consistency. The guests who belong look correct on opening night, equally correct three weeks later, and never behave as if the festival exists only for gala photographs.
seen_online: Online attention goes to opening-night foyers and polished arrivals. The better clue is whether someone appears across the season, not only once when the cameras are awake.
One elegant evening says attendance. Repeated patron context says habit.
If the post looks less like a premiere and more like an annual ritual, it probably is.

occasions.name: The Championships, Wimbledon
occasions.access_type: Public tickets, debentures, hospitality, private hosting
occasions.dress_code: Polished summer dressing; tailored and understated
occasions.guest_mix: Tennis patrons, corporate hosts, London society, global visitors
occasions.summary: Wimbledon remains the most mannered Grand Slam, where queues, debentures, and private hosting all coexist under a distinctly English standard of composure.
occasions.why_it_matters: It is a public event with private-club instincts. Ease, moderation, and correct summer dressing carry more weight than overt luxury.
occasions.the_room: Members, debenture holders, hospitality guests, tennis purists, and international visitors sharing a public championship that still runs on club instincts.
dress_notes: Summer tailoring and dresses should feel neat, breathable, and never beach-adjacent.
A blazer, cardigan, or light coat is often useful even on warm days when the weather turns.
Keep bags compact and security-friendly.
what_to_get_right: Know whether you are queueing, on debentures, in hospitality, or hosted privately; the rhythms differ.
Keep movement and conversation low during play, even in social areas near show courts.
Do not mistake a relaxed English tone for informality; staff and regulars notice standards.
before_you_go: Confirm gate, ticket type, and any lunch, tea, or member arrangements before arrival.
Plan for security, walking time, and possible rain delays.
Book post-tennis meals early if you intend to stay in town; the surrounding schedule fills quickly.
occasions.preview_summary: Wimbledon remains the most mannered Grand Slam, where queues, debentures, and private hosting all coexist under a distinctly English standard of composure.
occasions.premium_briefing: Wimbledon is a public championship with private-club reflexes: debenture holders, member guests, and hospitality patrons move on slightly different tracks, and nothing looks more off than forcing glamour into a room built on understatement.
seen_online: The internet gets strawberries, whites, and Centre Court. The better clue is whether the post quietly suggests debenture, hospitality, or member access.
A grounds photo says a lovely day. A properly placed lunch says another category entirely.
The most convincing Wimbledon flex is understatement. Grass and confidence do most of the work.

occasions.name: Henley Royal Regatta
occasions.access_type: Public enclosures, Stewards' Enclosure, private hospitality
occasions.dress_code: Summer club dressing; jackets and ties still matter in the right places
occasions.guest_mix: Rowing families, club members, London society, corporate hosts
occasions.summary: Henley blends competitive rowing with one of the most recognisable codes of the English summer, where the riverbank doubles as clubland in daylight.
occasions.why_it_matters: Henley is not about dramatic display. It is about understanding enclosure codes, river etiquette, and the art of appearing entirely at ease in heat and crowds.
occasions.the_room: Stewards, rowing alumni, club families, hospitality hosts, and London summer regulars who can read enclosure literacy at a glance.
dress_notes: Check enclosure rules carefully, especially for jackets, ties, hems, and hats.
Linen is welcome; sloppiness is not.
Choose footwear for grass, gravel, and towpaths, not polished floors alone.
what_to_get_right: Know whether you are public side, hospitality, or Stewards' Enclosure; behaviour changes with the badge.
Stand clear when crews, officials, and stewards are moving through tight riverside spaces.
Let the day keep its riverbank tempo; over-amped festival energy always reads imported.
before_you_go: Confirm enclosure passes, lunch plans, and any launch or river arrangements before the day begins.
Book riverside dining and transport early.
Carry weather cover that respects the dress code rather than defeating it.
occasions.preview_summary: Henley blends competitive rowing with one of the most recognisable codes of the English summer, where the riverbank doubles as clubland in daylight.
occasions.premium_briefing: Henley looks relaxed only to people who miss the codes. The real test is whether you understand enclosure rules, riverbank pacing, and the quiet hierarchy between rowing families, hosts, and summer spectators.
seen_online: Most coverage leans toward riverbank elegance and summer dressing. The better clue is which enclosure, host table, or launch arrangement keeps appearing in the background.
A striped blazer says Henley. The correct badge says which Henley.
If someone looks as though they arrived by habit rather than discovery, that is usually accurate.

occasions.name: Bayreuth Festival
occasions.access_type: Public tickets, patron allocations, invited receptions
occasions.dress_code: Formal evening dressing with cultural sobriety
occasions.guest_mix: Opera devotees, patrons, cultural figures, German and international guests
occasions.summary: Bayreuth is pilgrimage as much as festival. Guests arrive for Wagner, but the code of conduct is really about reverence for the institution and the room around it.
occasions.why_it_matters: Bayreuth rewards cultural seriousness and punishes affectation. It is one of the few places where knowing the room is inseparable from knowing the work.
occasions.the_room: Patrons, Wagner devotees, German cultural figures, donors, and annual guests who still treat attendance as a serious ritual rather than casual summer entertainment.
dress_notes: Think elegant evening dress with cultural sobriety rather than performance.
Comfort matters because programmes are long and the approach to the Festspielhaus is part of the evening.
Formal shoes should manage steps, summer heat, and extended intervals.
what_to_get_right: Arrive early; Bayreuth does not suit rushed entrances up the hill.
Reserve strong opinions for the interval conversation and deliver them at civilized volume.
Treat the ritual quality of the evening seriously even if your hosts are personally relaxed.
before_you_go: Check the work, running time, and seating before the day.
Plan for warmth, long durations, and limited spontaneity in supper timing.
Arrange transport back from the Festspielhaus in advance.
occasions.preview_summary: Bayreuth is pilgrimage as much as festival. Guests arrive for Wagner, but the code of conduct is really about reverence for the institution and the room around it.
occasions.premium_briefing: Bayreuth rewards cultural seriousness and preparation. The guests who look most at ease are usually the ones who have allowed for the hill, the length of the work, and the fact that conversation here assumes genuine interest rather than social filler.
seen_online: Bayreuth is quieter online than most of this calendar. The stronger signal is a post about the house, the work, or the ritual, not the person holding the phone.
A beautiful evening photo says culture. A thoughtful nod to the performance says commitment.
At Bayreuth, posting a little less can actually make you look as though you know more.

occasions.name: Monaco Yacht Show
occasions.access_type: Trade passes, buyer registration, hospitality, invitations
occasions.visibility: Trade and invitation-led with public access layers
occasions.dress_code: Marine-aware smart dressing; polished but mobile
occasions.guest_mix: Brokers, owners, shipyards, family offices, luxury partners
occasions.summary: The Monaco Yacht Show is a sales floor disguised as Riviera theatre. Access, punctuality, and restraint matter more than any attempt to perform wealth.
occasions.why_it_matters: A yacht show is built around introductions and trust. Guests who ignore crew, timing, or deck etiquette make themselves memorable for the wrong reasons.
occasions.the_room: Brokers, principals, family offices, shipyard teams, designers, and luxury partners conducting business at social volume along Port Hercule.
dress_notes: Choose pieces that move easily between pontoons, boardings, and hotel meetings.
Heels, bags, and jackets need to work on decks and docks.
Keep the palette calm; the harbour already supplies the spectacle.
what_to_get_right: Never board without explicit invitation from the host team or broker.
Treat crew with the same courtesy you offer owners and brokers.
Keep meetings on time; every serious guest is working a tightly layered schedule.
before_you_go: Map yacht visits carefully because walking times between berths are deceptive.
Carry credentials visibly and keep host names, berth numbers, and appointment slots straight.
Book lunches and late drinks early; Monaco fills around the show.
occasions.preview_summary: The Monaco Yacht Show is a sales floor disguised as Riviera theatre. Access, punctuality, and restraint matter more than any attempt to perform wealth.
occasions.premium_briefing: The show looks theatrical, but the useful currency is trust. Guests who respect crew, timing, and onboard etiquette are remembered as principals; guests who treat every deck like a photo set are remembered at all, which is worse.
seen_online: Most posts are pure harbour glamour. The better clue is whether someone is clearly on a named yacht for a meeting, not just wandering the dock with intent.
A stern shot says Monaco. An onboard appointment says business.
If the deck photo comes with a broker or host in frame, the post is doing less pretending.

occasions.name: Singapore Grand Prix
occasions.access_type: Grandstand, hospitality, suites, invited hosting
occasions.dress_code: Heat-smart evening dressing with city polish
occasions.guest_mix: Regional business leaders, F1 partners, hospitality guests, international travellers
occasions.summary: Singapore's night race is one of the cleanest combinations of serious hospitality and city-state precision on the Formula 1 calendar.
occasions.why_it_matters: Guests who dress for the humidity, respect timing, and move efficiently through hospitality zones stand out for the right reasons.
occasions.the_room: Regional business leaders, sponsor hosts, F1 guests, and international travellers moving between suites, clubs, and late-night city dinners.
dress_notes: Dress for humidity first and for photography second.
Tailoring should breathe; stiffness looks worse by the hour.
Evening polish works best when it remains functional across stairs, shuttles, and crowded hospitality decks.
what_to_get_right: Move early between zones; the circuit crowds quickly after dusk.
Be exact about guest credentials, gate numbers, and suite access.
Singapore service standards are high, so impatience is especially revealing.
before_you_go: Check gate, zone, and transport arrangements before leaving the hotel.
Hydrate and dress with weather in mind; the city extracts heat from poor planning.
Plan late suppers around traffic and post-race congestion.
occasions.preview_summary: Singapore's night race is one of the cleanest combinations of serious hospitality and city-state precision on the Formula 1 calendar.
occasions.premium_briefing: Singapore rewards clean logistics. The guest who arrives dry, on time, and correctly credentialed into the right suite looks far more impressive than the one wearing the better jacket.
seen_online: Online Singapore is neon skyline, paddock glamour, and night-race mood. The better clue is whether the post shows the right suite, zone, or host context.
A dramatic Marina Bay photo says atmosphere. Being moved smoothly through the evening says status.
If someone still looks composed in the humidity, they deserve a minor medal.

occasions.name: PAD London
occasions.access_type: Public tickets, collectors' previews, invited events
occasions.dress_code: Mayfair day dressing with collector polish
occasions.guest_mix: Collectors, dealers, designers, advisers, social buyers
occasions.summary: PAD brings design, decorative arts, and collector conversation into a setting that rewards taste, patience, and well-judged daytime polish.
occasions.why_it_matters: Art-fair manners are largely about not treating every stand as a backdrop or every dealer as a shop assistant.
occasions.the_room: Collectors, advisers, dealers, decorators, designers, and socially fluent buyers moving between stands, lunches, and Mayfair appointments.
dress_notes: Daywear should feel polished enough for Mayfair but easy enough for long hours on foot.
Quiet tailoring and excellent accessories read particularly well here.
A heavy-logo approach feels out of place among serious collectors.
what_to_get_right: Let dealers finish with clients before stepping into a conversation.
Ask before photographing works, booths, or interiors.
Do not use a stand as a backdrop for long social chatter when others are viewing.
before_you_go: Secure preview access early if you want first look at strong pieces.
Review exhibitors before you arrive; it changes the quality of the day.
Plan lunches and meetings around Frieze-week traffic, not despite it.
occasions.preview_summary: PAD brings design, decorative arts, and collector conversation into a setting that rewards taste, patience, and well-judged daytime polish.
occasions.premium_briefing: PAD is less hectic than Frieze and therefore less forgiving of sloppy manners. The strongest guests look carefully, speak economically, and understand that design buying often begins with trust rather than immediate enthusiasm.
seen_online: Most posts focus on beautiful stands and elegant objects. The better clue is preview-hour access or an actual conversation with a dealer.
A stylish browse says attendance. Looking closely before the crowd arrives says purpose.
If the post has fewer selfies and better furniture, someone probably came with a plan.

occasions.name: Frieze London & Frieze Masters
occasions.access_type: Public tickets, previews, VIP access, invited events
occasions.dress_code: Polished city daywear with gallery-floor practicality
occasions.guest_mix: Collectors, galleries, curators, patrons, luxury partners
occasions.summary: Frieze week turns London into a dense circuit of fairs, dinners, previews, and private viewings, with Regent's Park as the obvious centre of gravity.
occasions.why_it_matters: Art-fair etiquette is about looking, listening, and not mistaking visibility for importance. Good guests leave room for dealers to do their work.
occasions.the_room: Collectors, galleries, advisers, museum figures, patrons, and a broader social ring trying not to look like one.
dress_notes: Think urban polish that survives long hours and changing weather.
Clothes should not compete with the art or the dealer's client conversation.
Footwear matters more than anyone admits across the tents, previews, and dinners.
what_to_get_right: Preview access is a privilege; use it calmly and keep dealer time brief when traffic is heavy.
Ask before photographing works or people on stands.
Build space into conversations; the fair runs on short, repeated meetings rather than one long performance.
before_you_go: Plan a route across both fairs and your off-site viewings; Frieze week rewards discipline.
Coordinate lunches and dinners well ahead because London fills fast.
If collecting seriously, review exhibitor lists and hold priorities before arrival.
occasions.preview_summary: Frieze week turns London into a dense circuit of fairs, dinners, previews, and private viewings, with Regent's Park as the obvious centre of gravity.
occasions.premium_briefing: Frieze week is won by routing, not posturing. The guests who do it well know which stands require an early pass, which dinners matter more than the fair floor, and when to stop talking so a dealer can close a sale.
seen_online: Online Frieze becomes one long blur of tents, dinners, and people trying to look accidentally interesting. The better clue is early preview access and specific dealer context.
A crowded aisle says you were there. A calm stand before the rush says you were there correctly.
If someone seems to have a route instead of a wander, the week is probably working in their favor.

occasions.name: Abu Dhabi Grand Prix
occasions.access_type: Grandstand tickets, paddock hospitality, yacht and suite hosting
occasions.dress_code: Warm-weather evening polish with hospitality precision
occasions.guest_mix: F1 guests, luxury clients, regional hosts, international travellers
occasions.summary: Abu Dhabi closes the Formula 1 year with a hospitality calendar that stretches well beyond the track and rewards guests who can move through a busy weekend without looking hurried.
occasions.why_it_matters: Final-race weekends gather more expectation than most. The room values polish, punctuality, and a strong sense of who is hosting whom.
occasions.the_room: Team guests, principals, sponsor hosts, regional luxury clients, and an end-of-season crowd that expects polished handling as standard.
dress_notes: Keep evening looks refined and climate-aware.
Choose footwear that works across circuit, suite, yacht, and after-party settings.
Polish beats excess, especially in sponsor and family-hosted areas.
what_to_get_right: Treat credentials and invitation tiers carefully; access is exact and monitored.
Be on time for hosted arrivals, especially when boats, shuttles, or escorted transfers are involved.
Match the host's tone; some rooms are celebratory, others are still transactional.
before_you_go: Map the circuit, marina, and hospitality entries before race weekend begins.
Confirm transport details for Yas Marina evening events well ahead.
Build in recovery time; the final-race social calendar runs later than most guests admit.
occasions.preview_summary: Abu Dhabi closes the Formula 1 year with a hospitality calendar that stretches well beyond the track and rewards guests who can move through a busy weekend without looking hurried.
occasions.premium_briefing: As the season finale, Abu Dhabi gathers more expectation than most grands prix. The best guests move smoothly between circuit hospitality and marina evenings without ever making the staffing, transport, or invitation choreography look difficult.
seen_online: Most posts go straight to fireworks, marina parties, and race-week glamour. The better clue is whether the evening seems properly hosted from circuit to dinner.
A waterfront celebration says finale. Smooth movement between hospitality layers says access.
If the night looks effortless, that usually means a lot of staff worked very hard in the background.

dining_topics.title: Cutlery and Place Settings
dining_topics.tagline: Reading the table before the first course

sections.heading: The Outside-In Rule
sections.body: Work from the outermost cutlery inward. Implements furthest from the plate are used first. When uncertain, observe the host and follow.

sections.heading: The Resting Position
sections.body: Between bites, place knife and fork in an inverted V on the plate, tines down. Once used, cutlery does not return to the tablecloth.

sections.heading: The Finished Position
sections.body: Knife and fork placed together diagonally, handles at four o'clock, signals the course is complete.

sections.heading: The Fish Knife
sections.body: The fish knife and fork are reserved for the fish course. The broad blade is not intended for meat.

dining_topics.title: Seating Protocol
dining_topics.tagline: Placement and precedence at the formal table

sections.heading: Seating by the Host
sections.body: At formal dinners, the host arranges all seats. Place cards sit above the central cutlery or on the folded napkin. Do not rearrange.

sections.heading: Precedence
sections.body: The guest of honour sits to the host's right, the second most senior to the left. Partners are customarily seated apart to encourage the table as a whole.

sections.heading: Being Seated
sections.body: Remain standing until invited to sit. Gentlemen may assist the lady on their right by drawing back her chair.

sections.heading: Leaving the Table
sections.body: Leave only in genuine necessity, and alert the host quietly. Departing during a toast is a discourtesy that will be noticed.

dining_topics.title: Wine Service
dining_topics.tagline: Accepting, declining, and the pace of the table

sections.heading: Accepting Wine
sections.body: Allow the glass to be filled if you do not intend to drink. A quiet word to the sommelier suffices. Inverting the glass is unnecessary and reads as theatrical.

sections.heading: Tasting the Wine
sections.body: The host tastes to check for fault, not to express preference. Rejecting a sound wine on grounds of personal taste is not appropriate.

sections.heading: Refills
sections.body: Service staff manage refills. Reaching across to fill another guest's glass is not done at a formal table.

sections.heading: Order of Service
sections.body: Dry before sweet, light before full. Champagne may open the meal or accompany it throughout. Dessert wines follow the pudding course.

sections.heading: Turning the Table
sections.body: The hostess sets the direction. She addresses the guest on her right first, then turns left. Guests observe and follow her lead at the appropriate moment.

sections.heading: Topics to Embrace
sections.body: Travel, the arts, literature, history and the occasion itself. Ask questions; listen with genuine interest.

sections.heading: Topics to Avoid
sections.body: Religion, partisan politics, personal finances, health and social controversy. If raised by others, proceed with restraint.

sections.heading: Interrupting
sections.body: Do not interrupt. When another speaks at length, listen fully and respond to what was said rather than steering the conversation elsewhere.

dining_topics.title: The Table Napkin
dining_topics.tagline: A small matter that is noticed

sections.heading: Placing the Napkin
sections.body: Place the napkin on the lap once the host has done so. Never tuck it into a collar or waistband.

sections.heading: Using the Napkin
sections.body: The napkin dabs the lips; it does not wipe them. Use it discreetly when required.

sections.heading: Leaving the Table
sections.body: When leaving temporarily, place the napkin loosely on the chair seat. On the table signals the meal is finished.

sections.heading: End of Meal
sections.body: At the close, place the napkin loosely to the left of the setting. A neatly refolded napkin implies the hospitality was not to one's satisfaction.

dining_topics.title: Bread and Rolls
dining_topics.tagline: Small courtesies at the bread basket

sections.heading: Breaking, Not Cutting
sections.body: Rolls are broken by hand into small pieces, not cut. Butter each piece individually before eating it.

sections.heading: The Bread Plate
sections.body: The bread plate sits to your left. When in doubt: food left, drink right. The mnemonic BMW serves well — Bread, Meal, Water.

sections.heading: Timing
sections.body: Bread should not displace the courses that follow. Take modestly; the kitchen has more to offer.

dining_topics.title: Common Dining Mistakes
dining_topics.tagline: What is noticed, and by whom

sections.heading: At the Start
sections.body: Eating before the host is served or has invited guests to begin. Unfolding the napkin before the host. Adjusting or rearranging the place setting.

sections.heading: During the Meal
sections.body: Elbows on the table while eating. Reaching across a neighbour. Seasoning without tasting first. Blowing on hot food. Speaking with a full mouth.

sections.heading: With Cutlery
sections.body: Gripping the knife as one would a pen, or the fork as a shovel. Scraping the plate. Returning used cutlery to the tablecloth. Selecting the wrong utensil.

sections.heading: With Drink
sections.body: Clinking glasses with force. Drinking with food in the mouth. Cradling the wine glass by the bowl. Drinking before the toast is finished.

sections.heading: At the Close
sections.body: Rising before the host. Stacking plates or clearing one's own setting. Attending to grooming at the table. Departing without conversation once the final course is done.

address_entries.title: The Sovereign
address_entries.usageNote: After the initial 'Your Majesty', 'Sir' (for a king) or 'Ma'am' (rhyming with 'jam', for a queen) thereafter. Do not extend your hand unless offered first.

address_entries.title: Prince of the Blood Royal
address_entries.role: Son or grandson of the sovereign
address_entries.writtenForm: To His Royal Highness The Prince of Wales
address_entries.usageNote: Thereafter 'Sir'. Gentlemen bow the head; ladies curtsy on first greeting and departure.

address_entries.title: Duke
address_entries.writtenForm: His Grace the Duke of Norfolk
address_entries.usageNote: Thereafter 'Duke'. The Duchess is likewise 'Your Grace', then 'Duchess'.

address_entries.title: Earl or Countess
address_entries.writtenForm: The Right Honourable the Earl of Devon
address_entries.usageNote: An Earl is 'Lord [Earldom]' in speech; Countess becomes 'Lady [Earldom]'. Children hold courtesy titles.

address_entries.title: Archbishop of Canterbury
address_entries.writtenForm: The Most Reverend and Right Honourable the Lord Archbishop of Canterbury
address_entries.usageNote: Likewise the Archbishop of York. Provincial usage may differ.

address_entries.title: Diocesan Bishop
address_entries.writtenForm: The Right Reverend the Lord Bishop of London

address_entries.title: Cardinal
address_entries.usageNote: Given name precedes 'Cardinal'; family name follows.

address_entries.title: Ambassador
address_entries.usageNote: Retained through posting and customarily thereafter. Commonwealth High Commissioners identical.

address_entries.title: Minister Plenipotentiary
address_entries.writtenForm: His Excellency Mr Jian Liu, Minister of the People's Republic
address_entries.usageNote: Chargés d'affaires a.i. hold equivalent courtesy during tenure.

address_entries.title: Field Marshal
address_entries.writtenForm: Field Marshal The Lord Dannatt GCB CBE MC
address_entries.usageNote: Rank held for life. Full designation precedes name in formal use.

address_entries.title: General
address_entries.writtenForm: General Sir Mark Carleton-Smith KCB CBE
address_entries.usageNote: Naval equivalent is Admiral. Rank precedes surname.

address_entries.title: Lord / Lady Justice of Appeal
address_entries.writtenForm: The Right Honourable Lord Justice Singh
address_entries.usageNote: In court: 'My Lord / My Lady'. Life peers where applicable.

address_entries.title: Professor
address_entries.usageNote: Reserved strictly for full professors, not lecturers.

address_entries.title: Knight Bachelor
address_entries.usageNote: Always first name: 'Sir James', never 'Sir Smith'. 'Dame Helen', never 'Dame Mirren'.
